speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Dick Benge took place at his home on Hatter Creek near Princeton on April 12, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I guess we strike out at these all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember when you talked about these oddities? Were there any winners in the early days that were real odd like that?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, you know, the first winner I was here, I came in 13 and that was a matter of 13 and 14. You know, we never got no snow with Christmas.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, it started until Christmas Eve. We got up next morning and had something to say. What in 90 days on a logging sled.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That was quite a winner.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Boy, oh boy. You know, the road was half the size of this house. It all froze and now all the snow on each side of it were gone. Well, she was just packing nothing but ice. That was a little bit of a mess.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, that was something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In that kind of snow weather, was it hard to get around or could you get around pretty easy?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, it was hard to get the road broke. You didn't break your road out the first thing you know you couldn't go unless you shoveled. She just keeps snowing and settling, snowing and settling wet, you know. Snowing and settling, snowing and settling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will you just break the road out with the team? Is that all?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That's all we had them days. We had no cats or nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to puff them a lot, to rest them a lot to break the road?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, quite a bit. Until you got your road course, you started snowing. You broke all your road out. That was the first thing you done. You'd break your road out, you'd load the light, go clerk the back end of your job and bring everything out as you come. Well, whenever you got done on that road, you were done with it. You'd snow it full if you wanted to. We'll go over there. No, we had no cats some days, no nothing, you know. In 1913 there was no cats. The company never got logging cats in here. I believe it was 1918 when they got cats. They got two of them, four of them old holds. I heard they used one on the park sleigh hall. They used cats for the last part of the hall. They used some of the old time cats on it. Well, the old, you know, they sold out and they sold to the best. Well, then the company got the best cats. Well, they used them a couple of years and then they sold out to the caterpillar. And it was all cats. All caterpillars went on and are yet practically international, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was them, the first ones that they brought in here, did they work very good, them early ones?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, they didn't. Because, where was Morgan? I really saw him up at 6 for 1. 2.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: And they couldn't keep them on the tracks. Because no rock rails, no nothing. Now they got rock rails, you know, while you can't throw it at the tracks. Run right over some sideways and it won't throw it off.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So, I guess they're better now. You know how they keep it going?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah. Keep it going now. Oh, this speed, you know. World's traveling fast, too fast. Don't you think?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I agree with you.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Just the traveling's fast, but we can't keep up with it. Well, where's it gonna stop?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: How you and I can go buy an airplane ticket and cross the ocean and go clear around the world, you know, in a little bit. What business have we got with that?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: We're furnishing the money to buy them, or tax us.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, so I can't see where we're gaining anything. Let me just go one further behind. Oh, I think you're right, Dick. I think you're right about that. Well, yeah, we're not gaining nothing. It's got to stop, but we're... I think we'll be right up against the wall. When it does. Where it looks now, you know, unless there's an oil change somewhere. You think when you was growing up, back in the early days, were people...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they want things like they do now?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No. No, they were satisfied with it. They didn't want an automobile, they didn't want this, they didn't want that. If the kids were to afford it, they got one, they couldn't have them. They got it. Let it go. Sure, satisfied. But nowadays, you know, this is where he gets something. His neighbor got to have one just like it, or a little better. Yeah.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Them days, they didn't. Once your neighbor got that, it was all right with you.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: It wasn't the kind of envy that there is now. Once, you know, reach out and grab it. I can grab it now. Yeah.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. Yeah, they got to have it. You know, they don't get it while they're not satisfied. Well, you take these young fellers, I don't know what they're going to do. They come out of college or they come out of high school. And they go get them a job. Well, they get their job on the way back or something by an automobile.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Let their job pay for it. If the job don't pay for it, it goes back.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: The way you see it going, by the gods, I guess it's all right. I don't know. I mean, you know, you say it was okay if you didn't care if a neighbor had much more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think everybody was in the same shape then? Or was there some people that were doing better than others?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That's right. There was some people better off. Oh, yeah. It was a real difference.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, someone was better off, you know. They got by a little better, but them days, you know, it didn't take so much good buying.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, you know, you could go to town and you could buy a sack of flour for a dollar and a half. Buy slab of meat, a big slab of bacon for four dollars. I don't know how much it weighed. Sax spuds for fifty cents to a dollar. Well, you didn't have to have them big rages to make it. No. You didn't take them. You didn't think nothing about the big rages. I tell you what I think, I think the working man is a putter on the hog as much as anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Working man is what?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Putting the whole country on the hog. Every time living goes up, they got a strike. The unions, they got a strike. Well, as quick as they strike, well, then living will go up to meet it. Well, living will go up, and they'll turn around and strike because they raised eleven. They got to have more money to make eleven. Well, then they'll turn around and raise the price of groceries again. But what about the... Maya, I always have a hard time with the idea of profits. I mean, it seems like the profit is kind of a rig off it. Why, yeah. Off the working man is how I feel about it. It is. It is. I tell you, I've worked out most of my life. And I've kind of figured out it all starts with the working man.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: The man that's working by the hour is purchasing all the money to keep things going.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, he is. He'll figure it right out and see if he ain't. And every time he shuts down or strikes, he ties it all up.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, it might be he's gone or runnin' it. How'd the truck strike come out?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The way I understood it, they went back. They went back to work. It didn't last long. Now, that's what I think, but I don't know if they were all back or not. Last week, my wife and I took a vacation. We kind of dropped out in the middle of it. We heard a part of it on the news. Then we didn't hear the news for three, four days. So, last I heard, there was at least a third back. And I haven't heard anything on the news about it lately, so I figure they're all back. No, they might be all back now. Anyhow, they got their transportation going.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, bad, then, when they strike. You know, I was the one to strike. I was in the strike in 1917. R .W .W.'s truck. Now, they didn't strike for more money. All they wanted was better conditions. They got them. Yeah, they wanted electric lights. They got the bathroom, washroom. All this is that. Steel bunks. And they wanted the company to furnish the bedding, and they did. They brung the company to time there. Well, why not?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Before you had to pay your suitings. And you didn't have no mattress to put it on. You got the can of old wooden bunks, bedbugs, and traybacks.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I know I slurred.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How bad were they, Dick?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, they were bad. I used to get taken to some of them old jacks. They'd be out there working long and on. There's something funny about them, the graybacks. One, he'd pick off one, he'd find one, and he'd pick it up. By God, I got one, I'll whip yours. Probably bet a dollar, dollar and a half, maybe two dollars. He'd get one, and they'd put them to fighting. You take one off you and one off me and put them two together, and they'll fight. It was fun to watch them fight.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: We had lots of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, you fight like that, would they really fight? Fight?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You bet they'd be. The red buckets, or the graybacks would fight to beat hell.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Did you ever see a green back? Huh? Did you ever see a green back?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, no, no. They were just human, no, it's just what they are. Well, I had one running with me, so... That's all they are, just a human louse, or a human being. That's what they call a grayback.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, there was no way that a man could work in the woods and not get them, was there?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Why, no, you couldn't. You couldn't work in the woods and not get them. Boy, then when you come home to see mother and dad, I'd put the clothes off at the woodshed and take a good nap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before they let you in the house?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, you bet. Of course, they know what to expect, so they'd be prepared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, was there much chance to bathe when you were in the woods then before they improved the conditions?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No. You know what, we fixed up the bay that the hole came to. We took one of them square, I suppose you've seen them, five gallon coal oil cams.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, we'd take and punch holes in the bottom of them. And we took gunnysack and built us a combat tent, drowned out in the one in the bunkhouse. We'd go up there and one fella, he'd stand up above and pour water in while the other fella took a bath.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: We'd pull the nails all out. Oh, well, we had lots of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pull the nails all out of?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: The holes, you know. Oh, yeah. We had holes so the water would run right on through the town. The shower we got.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, when you were there, Dan, was the guys complaining about that?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I mean, that sounds like a hell of a way to live, you know. Well, it was, but then he was all used to it, and then we were actually used to it there from Minnesota, Wisconsin, fair out here. And it hadn't gotten any better.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So, that's what...

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: What do you need, Daddy?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That's the way they got used to it, they was used to it.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: It's kind of like an outdoor toilet. It's never known any better. You don't expect any better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: It just sounds, it sounds like kind of rough. Yeah. Kind of rough. It is. Well, it's been breaking up all the time, but it's been broke up on these days. I'm talking about then, I'm talking about living in the, you know, without no good showers and with them bugs on you all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make a guy short -tempered, living that way?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, it was all short -tempered anyhow. The jacks mostly are. A little short -tempered. Don't take much to write them up. I wonder why, I wonder why that is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it the work that does it to the man?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: The work they do and being by their selves, you know. I was waiting there folks and everything. Everything kind of goes against the grain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, what were you going to say?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Go ahead. Oh, I was just thinking that, you know, I was thinking everybody living in the camp, I'm thinking of what it would be like. Everybody living there together, you have to bend some to, you know. Oh yeah, you've got to bend a little, you know. Of course, nowadays, you know, well, before, well, how long ago has it been to me since the ... moved into camp?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, I don't know why. About thirty years ago. In 36, when Camp 36 was up there, they started to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who started to?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: They started to move. Oh. The ... were moving in and out. And now then they have automobiles and they ride into camp. Well, they moved, they started having families living right in the camp. Right in the camp, yeah. The company they got to put them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many families lived here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you built the house?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: First. That was in 24. About eight families lived here in the marrying shack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now they had families here in the camp in 24?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh yeah. That is, they had marrying shacks and they were gone. Well, what was. We built this house there and the camp was over there two years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the difference between 24 and 36 for the families?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, more. And the families now like me, they got to buy and land off the company. See, building their own house on it. Well, they had a car and then drives worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this marrying shack, what kind of, how much space did a family have in something like that? I think about the 16 foot tower, wasn't it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Or about, no, there wasn't. 24 foot long and 12 feet wide. Because we built two of them to build this house. Well, them wasn't marrying shacks, them wasn't bunk houses. I don't know, oh, the marrying shacks, I don't know. I didn't know they had families living in camps back that far, back in the 20s. They got, you know, the jacks, they got married and got to buy and land off the company. They got to helping them build up, and the company did.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That's the way they got them settled up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think it softened the camps up at all, Dick?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, yeah, now they haven't got a camp. Well, no, I mean, to have the families living in there. Yeah, it did. It sounds like maybe it wouldn't be quite as rough a place if they had families. No, it wasn't.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I thought that most of the jacks were single men and never did get married in their earlier days. Well, that's right, yeah. Well, like everything else, it changed then. They got to getting married, you know. Moving around.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: With the camps, they'd move right along with the camp, you know.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: This short temper, short temper, you said. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Once when we was talking, you told me about Dick Farrell taking care of a couple of them lumberjacks, and I'm wondering, do you remember, was there other fights in the woods that you saw?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: None, see two of them get into it.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Lots more are nowadays. They kind of get along nowadays, but they didn't used to.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, you just had to be careful what you said and where you said it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would start more?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: What would be another... Almost anything.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Politics used to cost a lot of it. Boy, I had a couple of... Well, they had, oh, 12, I think, or 14 old men. Well, it was an election year, and the boss, he counted them in, he said, I'm sorry, Dick, I wish you'd take all them old fellas over, and I said, What's the matter now, Bob? And he said, More than I can handle. Well, he had them cutting landings. Well, they'd get into it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Politics, politics, are you?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Directly get the white one. Well, you'd have to take a match and go up and separate them, and get them faced. Well, you could take one over here on this end, they might be a mile apart. The other one down there, and it wouldn't be an hour until they'd be together and you arguing again. So, we had to that way, and it was the worst, and I went to the boss one night, and I said, Bob, you're going to log over across the creek this winter into the op.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, Why'd you say we go over and make some landings? He said, I don't know, Johnny, he said, What's the idea?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: And I told him. I wanted to separate them two men, so I could get some work done. Fine. So, the next morning I went out, and I stopped one of them and three others. And I said, You fellas, wait. We're going to take you over across behind the barn and start making landings over there, before it snows too much so we can log a little over there this winter. Of course, I didn't tell them why. Well, I took four. I'm trying to find another one. I don't know where. They didn't get together. They didn't know where one another was.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Boy, I'm going to argue. I wonder what the heck they were, what their argument was about. Well, politics. When I'm a Democrat and I'm a Republican, I turned it loose. I used sandal felt behind the tree and less madam old boy.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Neither one of them could ever probably convince the other one of the thing. No. Well, I had one, old Abe. We called him Abraham Lincoln. He looked just like Abraham Lincoln. Big, old, tall, old, grown fella.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: And he was way the smartest. You know, there was the smartest man that ever I saw, I believe. He'd take that spokesman review paper. You know, he'd come in. Well, we had about, oh, three quarters of an hour, an hour before supper. You know, he'd go along through that paper before supper. And we used to try to hem him up on us, you know. We'd pick out this little statue here and there, you know. You'd pick out one and I'd pick out one and we'd read it and we'd ask him about it. He could tell us and there was a word was in her, both of them.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: And the Bible, he knowed it upside down and backwards.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, he...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he use it? Did he talk it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He talked to her. Yeah. He moved down here to Princeton. And they shut down that winter. There was no such thing. They had to shut down and he moved down here and you ran him a little out.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: They had a church down there and no minister. So I was talking to Thomas and he said, He said, he got no minister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He done what talking to him?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, I done just as well as he done to get us. I told him about Abe. Abe lived right there in the neighborhood. I said, you go see Abe. He went up and seen him. Yeah, he went all the way down. Well, he'd go down every Sunday and preach a good sermon for him. Cause he knowed the Bible and he knowed just where everything was. But he didn't really consider himself to be a preacher or anything. No, but he believed in it. He was no minister, that is, no preacher, but he believed in it. Strong, very strong. Whenever you're a good old feller, never seen him get mad on in politics. He get pretty mad at that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty mad at what?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Politics. Oh, Abe did. Yeah, he wanted to be argued.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a Republican?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, he was a Democrat. Raised in the South.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Quite the old feller. That's funny that he'd just be able to memorize all that stuff. That seems like a... Well, I'll tell you what. He growed up in an apprentice shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A printer shop?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. His father was an apprentice. And when he got old enough, he'd ask over all the papers. And see, that word was spelt right. And boy, if he struck a word in the paper that wasn't spelt this right, he'd tell you about it right now. And it didn't take him all day to find it. Yeah. Yeah, we used to laugh. Poor old feller.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why say poor old feller? Why say poor old feller?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, he didn't have anything. He was just a very, very common working man.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I wonder...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he the one, Vic, that wouldn't take any money from preachers? Wouldn't take up no collection? He wouldn't take no collection?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Uh -huh. No, what he talked and spoke out of the Bible was all free with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever have any idea on how he went to the woods? Why he went to the woods instead of, say, newspaper?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, he was married and he had a boy about 16. I guess, 16, between 16 and 17 someplace. And he brung him to the woods with him. That way he could be with him. He was with him all the time to see that they didn't get into trouble and raising him right. So that's the reason why they were woken up in the woods together. Well, it was a good idea.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Very, very nice boy. Well -mannered boy.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You know, when... If a guy got knocked down, he could expect that another guy was going to stomp on him with his cork boots. Oh, he'd be a lover, if not, yeah. Yep, you bet. That's the way they done her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would a man hold a grudge very long in the woods?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, they didn't hold a grudge very long. If they were whipped, he'd be whipped. That's about all there was to it. He's a better man. I heard it said that at camp that a man would get to be known as a bully of the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that so, too?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He'd want to take anybody on? Did he want to try them?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, I bet. But he'd run into some little feller and take it all out of him right now.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I see he had one of them up there around Whirlpool. Oh, boy, he was a bully and he whipped several. The lorries would come in there.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So, I think the Irishman would come in and just whip him. He went downtown one night and I got drinking a little and I got into her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Little Irishman backed off and looked at him and he said, Are you ready? Ready for you anytime?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He said, Look out, I'm coming. And he just gave a hop step and a jump and kicked him right in the face with both feet. Turned the flip -flop backwards and sat there looking at him, waiting for him to get up. But he didn't drunk him any. But he kicked him down three times that way.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So, that was plenty. Well, he just couldn't dodge him either. That little feller was, I don't know, I never seen a man just as catty as he was on his feet. He never used his fists. But he sure took it out of the bullet. Little Irishman, he was a little cook of the woods.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He was a little guy too. They didn't argue.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he stay around the woods for a while?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, he was around the woods there for, I don't know, he was on there for, I guess a couple of years. That's all trouble he ever did have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They wouldn't take him on then?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh no, they didn't want him. Okay. Well, he was, uh, Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you doing, drinking that again?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, I gotta go get some of this. Yeah, Herschel was telling me about them, some of them guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there guys from Austria, Austrians in the woods there?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh yeah, it was Austrians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what he calls the Bohunks or is that another guy?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, there's anything that come across that wasn't very plain, couldn't talk very plain, he called him Bohunk. But, uh, them Austrians, now we worked, Herschel was working and I was still in there. Can't fire. He was, uh, I see he was, you know, it was camp five. Then went over to camp ten. Herschel and I, we got a pig in there one day. And, uh, one Sunday, we just up and I. He was, uh, eighteen or nineteen, I think, poverty. None of them related. All from Austria. All Austrians. And it was a while there that they had, uh, John Kvavich, he was an interpreter for them. Boy, would he cut them up one side and down the other sometimes. He wanted to make them talk our language. And if he happened to step in the bunkhouse and there's a jabber in the way of their language, boy, it didn't take him long to cut up. He said, you fellers in the United States now, you talk American. He said, these fellers may think you were talking about them when I'm gonna get up and swat you directly. Said, they can't understand our language. Don't do it. And he got them pretty well all broke. But I used to get tickled out of them eating some of them fellers or just those in the old country and all. He'd get them pork and ham and this ham and I'd ham them and this ham and I'd get them this way and I'd eat with their hams.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You know what that pork and ham and pork are for. I don't know, I was gonna ask John a few times if they had them in the old country and oh, I suppose they did, yes. And I didn't want to embarrass him either because he's a very nice fellow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they eat, would you just eat off the knife?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, they wouldn't use a knife or fork. They'd hold them, the handles on the handle this way and hold them down on the handles and he'd eat it this way like watermelon. Yeah. I see, I see. They'd use a knife and fork just to hold the meat. Like them things you use on the end of corn. I don't know what the poor devil's doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now do them guys, do them guys, do you think they had a rougher time than the American lumberjacks did? What was it like for them?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, they made good jacks. It was all good jacks.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Get them all jobs. Of course, they'd done their brother Ihut turned around with them in Austria. One that could talk to the United States, you know. And they'd put him out with them, that way he could train them and learn them. What they wanted done. Yeah, it was some awful good ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was one of them, what was it called him?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: The saddest man I've ever seen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Saddest?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He was, he was short every set. And he was bow -legged. And, you know, Bo Bach, on a ground liner, weighs 700 pounds. And we was moving our ground liner one day and we was talking and we didn't know he was the stuff, we'd never seen him lifted him. So one of them come along and he said, what's his name, I can't think of it. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said to him, he said, can you pick up that Bo Bach today?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yes, sir, he said I can.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So he got a straddle of it and took a PB handle and run through the yoke. He picked her off the ground.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Now that's a pretty good lifting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, if he'd got ahold of a man, he'd just crush him, wouldn't he?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Or could if he'd wanted to. He did that by himself. By himself, yeah. It only took three men to move it.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Of course, now, he didn't pass it, he just picked it up off the ground. And he got the straddle of it to do it, but then that was something. But that's what they weighed, 700 pounds of yoke and shiv and the whole thing.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Boy, they must have had a lot of respect for him. Well, he was the best human man you ever saw. Oh, he's had a grin on his face, I never seen him the other way. Put a grin on his face. Say anything to him and he'd probably grin before he'd answer you. But he always had that grin on him. I guess he didn't take no special pride in it then, maybe. People around trying to show it off or anything. No, he didn't. We didn't know he could do that when the boys come along and worked with him for a while. We didn't know if he could do it. I said, yeah, Mr. Curb. Ask you why you don't like garlic. They says they'd ask you why you don't like garlic. Oh, the cook I was working in the kitchen. Garlic, you know, is quite a handle of mess with the meats and stuff, you know. So he sent me a whole piece of it. Well, the store room was right next to the office and the office was... The store room was between the office and the dining room. Jesus, they got into that garlic. Them fellers found that and they got into the garlic there the night. For breakfast. I got sick, I had to wait in tables and I had to go outdoors. I was all over the place just smelling that grass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, this was the Austrian fellas doing it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, boy, they just peeled that garlic and eat it like we do onions. They said that that's their raised lots over there. I wonder if they thought it was good for them or if they'd just done it for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it cause a lot to them?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I'll never know. I didn't think enough of a task.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You really couldn't hardly take it, the smell, huh?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, I couldn't take it. Oh, their brass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do with the garlic? What did you do with the garlic?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, sir, I took it between the dining room and the wear room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the what?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Between the dining room and the wear room and they all sat on the cars, you know, regular cars. And the snow was oh, about six, seven feet deep around the camp. I just drove off between the cars and jumped out on it. I guess it's there yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He goes along one day and he says, Dick, you know where the garlic's at?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You bet ya.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I said, it's out there between the dining room and the... ...wear room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who put it there?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I said, I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, why'd you do that for?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: In Christ, he said, that cost a lot of money. I said, I don't give a damn what it cost. I can't smell it. So he just laughed and said, thank you for doing what you did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said there was snow on the ground then?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh boy. Yeah, so they just lost it all. About five feet around the camp there. I got rid of the garlic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any friends among them guys?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, yeah. You know, I went in the camp and went in to work in the kitchen. The second night I was there, I went in where they... ...they'd all go down and play poker. Well, I went in where they was playing poker. Of course, they seen me there at the table and they got acquainted with me. So they wanted me to play poker, I don't know how. They got no money. We'll owe you the money. That's just the way they was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know how to play poker?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yes, I know how, but I wasn't going to the inassemble and playing poker.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No. Never did. They're too good for me.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Boy, there's some real poker players among them. Well, that's the way, all the way they got to spending money. Some of them old jacks. Maybe they wouldn't go out in town, you know, for three months, four months. Maybe a whole year before they ever go to town. Well, they had to spend the money and pass the time somewhere. So that's where they spent their playing poker.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty good sums turn over?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Some of them are good at it, they're good. One of them, he's a him and a Swede in the North Widgen. It was the three best poker players in the West in camp. They always wound up with the money somewhere.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: The Swede, he worked in the kitchen little now and then. If we run short of help, it ought to get him in there. He's good help in the kitchen. Boy, could he play poker. He lived over here in Moscow. So him and his brother come in there and soft. Well, he had a pretty good way to work. He'd come in about, oh, four or five days, four or five days. Well, he'd say, that payday and he'd get their money. Well, he'd say, two more months, two more paydays. Him and his brother saw him lost. And he'd clean up on them and he'd go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He come in there after he'd been helping in the kitchen and he said, anything you fellas want from town?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: It rained for five days straight and the boys never left the bunkhouse. All they done was play poker, day and night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Jesse come in and he said, anything you fellas want from town?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, you going to town?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. He said, I got to go to town. Well, back there in the wear room, he said, I'll show you something. We went back there and he had 15 of the boys' checks. And he had a roll bill to, well, I couldn't reach around. He said, I got to go to town, you put this in the bank. And he's with a woman's boy and his brother was with him and he didn't know what that card was. He couldn't pay for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His brother?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. Old Jesse. Boy, now he know how to pay for it. Last I know of him is Durkin's. Boy in Spokane. Jimmy Durkin. Well, he's a dealer in there in the card game. That's the last I ever heard of Jesse. Sure. That'd be nice. Well, I got to ask you something about this. If a guy was a shark, he'd come into camp. I mean, he knew how to go gamble. He was better than the rest of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could he keep getting away with it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I mean, I think maybe it'd work once, but geez, I would never get in a game with a guy who I knew was going to finish me off in one way or the other. Well, here's the idea. There's a number guy sitting there, Bill Abbott. And I think, well, my big brother, God, I can beat him this time. Play it out with him. Of course, now you take them good card players. They're onto that too. Long way. They let them have him now and then, you know.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You try and drink that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If he drinks his, you drink yours, okay?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: A little hot. We'll cool down a little bit here. You got to cool down for a second. Yeah. Of course, he's just one of us. I see him get $600 one time on a pair of jacks.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Now, that takes guts. No, you ain't got those for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, that takes real bluffing power. That's what he had. Well, he'd bluff time or two and they'd catch him up on it. He'd laugh it off with them. Jesus Christ, the next time no one would look out, they might have them. Yeah. Yeah. If he can get $600. He showed his hand down. The fella that bet against him, he said,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's your openers?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, my God, he said, I never showed them to him. He said, it's a pair of jacks. That's all it takes, you know, to open a box. So, he got down and got in his hand and turned his jacks over and that's all he had to do was show them. He didn't show the rest of his cards. That's all he had in his hand. Bluffed her all the way through.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So, that's the way he got by. On a lot of, and sometimes I've seen him throw down a good hand. He'd throw down a good hand sometimes, you know. Well, that's good.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, what the other fella had, I don't know, but it wouldn't be as bright as that. He had to beat. But, he couldn't take every hand. No. That's the way he had to get by with it.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Keep it from the boys on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For you yourself, before, did it make any difference to you before you was married? Would you spend, be willing to spend money gambling then or take a chance on them then?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, there was four of us. Five of us. Blow four horses down here in one winter, the other lower hand on the raise. We had a panty -andy game. Yeah. Two bits is all you could bet as an animal. We played all winter. I think I come out a dollar and six bits ahead.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. Yeah. We played all winter and I think I had a dollar and six bits when I counted up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think, Dick? Just because you didn't have that gambling instinct?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You didn't want to... I didn't want to get interested too heavily in it. Because, here's the idea, you know, there's so many that... Yeah.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Thank you. You're welcome.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I've seen so many, you know, there's one... There was two that went up there and they... worked their camp six. They were JPO in there. Geez, they paid them off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They got, what was it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Pretty close, four thousand dollars apiece. They got down for doing such right there in camp and played it all away. And they both had families. And I know their families could use it. And they come to me, Charlie Hawkins was one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He come to me and he said, could I get a guard game here?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: And I said, yeah. I said, listen Charlie, I'm your friend. I'm going to tell you something. I said, there's two of them here that leave alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, why?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: And I said, they'll get you. I said, they don't do it together. They just outplay you. I said, you're not good enough for them. He'd play with them. Well, I said, it's your money. I said, listen Charlie, your family needs it. Yeah, I said, I guess that's right. Damn it, if he didn't sit right there for... It took him two days and a night, or two nights and a day. I don't know what it was to get rid of it, but he got it. Sit right there and played it all away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He come into the camp with all that money?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, right there where they paid him off. See. So he had all that money coming to him and he gambled it away. Yeah, and he just gambled it away. Made it along as I wondered it along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those two guys got it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Them two guys got it. I don't know which one got the most.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could a man go home to his wife?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That's it, that's what I wondered. I've seen so much of that happen that that one thing kind of broke me from playing. I didn't want to keep playing and get the fever.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, if you take a penny any game, you can play all... take ten dollars and play all winter on that.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You know, you was talking about... you was telling me about Potlatch Joel some before. And I was thinking about him. I don't understand... was he just a kind of... He was an old Austrian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a smart fella?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No. He just... He just... Not too strong, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think about playing poker?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One time?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I was pretty good. About ever. He worked for Tom Kelly. I worked for Kelly quite a bit. About ever saw a man like Tom and he'd go out and give him a check or two. Now you go in Spokane and spend this in combat. It would work. So he'd slip in. Wouldn't tell him the job but quit in time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He'd slip in? You mean he wouldn't go to Spokane?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, he'd just slip in to camp. And the boss would go get a couple or three checks. He had sixteen checks there for him at one time. So he took a couple of them out to get them. Now he said, you take these and go to Spokane. Well, he had me go down in Jitpot, I feel, and bring them up to office. Well, it was time for the logger to move out to go load logs. They were going down to Derry and Kelly told him, he said, you can go down to Derry and catch the train out of there at noon. Yep. Oh, fella, he didn't do that. He rode the train out. Well, they went to Derry and then they went on down to Harshes Camp. He drove up. He went down there and got him a job and paid his checks away.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, brother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why wouldn't he go to Spokane? He just wanted to gamble in the camp instead?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He wanted to stay in the camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Kelly looking out for him?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I mean, was he... Well, in a way.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, Don could get more out of there than anybody.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yep. I guess I got that. She got this all hooked up her house. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you want to do?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I got to go take a leak. Oh, go ahead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The men had made them?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. They'd sent them to their posts and their rockers. And that's all they bought. They'd go up and down along the creek with the willers. God, they'd pick out just what they wanted, you know, and ride. So I didn't know. Well, they'd get what they wanted for a year and then they could build up as they went along. See the, you know, little stuff for the tops.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you be able to actually make some money selling them?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, you would. Well, I don't know if they made much money, but they made 11. For the winners' work. Yeah, sure. They'd make them all winners and they'd have 15, 20 of them. More concerts. I guess what they wanted a piece for, it wasn't too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they sell them right local or would they ship them straight?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, well, most of them, you know, they'd just take a team and an old hat and go around and sell them and then go around and deliver them and go around and sell them. That's the way they went. That sounds like a real craft, you know, to do it. Yeah, it does. Do it good. Nice job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first start to play violin?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, I don't remember. Dad played one before me. I used to get out and so on up and on and so forth.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Started in.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Of course, he showed me a lot about, he tuned my violin for me for a while. That was the main thing. He'd run them damn things to him. Boy, they're hard to tune. You get on to it and you get on to it and nothing to it.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, I think of violin, the prettiest music it is. You take a violin and they haven't invented a piece yet that you couldn't play on it. Somebody could play it on it, you know.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, it's real good music. It sounds so good, so pretty. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn from him then? Did he teach you how to play? Did your father teach you how to play?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, no, he didn't teach me how. I used to just watch him. And then, of course, he just played for a year old time, you know. Old timer.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. That's the worst trouble with a fella learning to play a violin is getting to tune it. That's the hardest part of it, is to get it to tune.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You know, when I got four strings, it looked like it'd be easy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: It really isn't, though. Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when did you start playing the violin when you were still a boy?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, yeah. I think I was only about 13, 14 when I took off. Well, he had none, and he sold his, traded it off or something, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the one you got in Spokane?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, it's the one I got now.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He bought one, and I don't know what happened. I think the kids got a hold of it, the grandkids. Got down one day off the wall, and nobody there. They got to playing it, and they busted it. We never did get a fix, then I was the folk handling one. The one's won us all. I bought me one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of stuff did you play?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Just old, just old dance music. Just the old -time dance music. That's all I played. Now don't know one note from one other. That is on paper.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, I never learned one note from another. Well, I got sorted, and then it was a fellow that used to come to our place lots. He put a good on one, and he told me, he said, Dick, you never should have learned to play to be your wife. He said, you'll never learn to play by note. So I found out he was right. I never tried it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you could play what you wanted to play, what's the difference?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You might be better off playing by hearing it. Well, you know, here's the idea. There's lots of them, you know, a little higher up, you know. Lots of them, they want this note stuff. Well, if you can't play it, well, that throws you out of their class. You know.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So that didn't bother me a bit. I didn't figure wanting to go up that high enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean, the main thing, if you were playing music, wouldn't it be mostly parties, dances, and that kind of thing?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just be local folks and having a good time, right?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That's all you know.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Just having a good time around in the country.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You're all doing the Depression. There's four of us in here. We could all play the violin. And there was, they had two sisters and I had one sister. And all of us could chord on an organ.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Boy, we go to these dances, you know, and well, we had no money. Sometimes they'd take up a collection for a bunch of us and all we wanted was this money and I'd buy strings. And we got money and I'd have to buy strings all winter. And that's just about all we got. Well, we didn't want any more, so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dances, Dick, would they be at your house and at their house? Is that where they'd be?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, they used to come here and dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many folks would come at a shot at a time?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, there's a whole neighborhood here. I don't know where they was.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: About,

speaker: Dick Benge
words: oh, I'd say there was 12, 15 families all hung together, you know. We had any flour, we'd, women would bake a cake. But then we'd go without it. That was during the Depression, you know. Well, we all went and had a good time. Depression didn't bother us that well. But I'm having a good time, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want some of this coffee? You got a taste for it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I got some here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess this is mine, isn't it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: It's cooling down. It's getting almost lukewarm now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How close do you think the neighborhood was?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I mean, the families were one to another. Oh, they were awful close then. Yeah, they were close, very close, you know. But the families nowadays is not that close. I just got it out here. You got automobiles now if you want to see your navy, you got to go to Lewiston or Spokane. But in those days, I mean, I'm trying to think of what it was like. I wasn't, you know, I've seen before my time now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, how was it, what was it that made it close?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, no money. They couldn't go noplace. And they had to get up there on amusement, you know. Well, that's the way it was. They just had to make their own amusement, you know, right at home.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, that's what it was. But nowadays, you know, you got to go to Spokane or Lewiston and take in one of them big something's. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that, would that mean you knew, that you knew the neighbors better? Why?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You know everybody. Yeah, you know everybody, them days, you know.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Now, these folks that lived down there in that trade house, they've been in there for five or six months now and I've never met them. Well, that wouldn't have happened in all them times. It all went down, three or four families went down some night. Got acquainted with them. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But not nowadays, huh?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Nowadays, you go down there and maybe it wouldn't be the home. Yeah, maybe be in the car and gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that mean like in them days that a family, that the families would look after and look out for the neighbors and the neighbors kids?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, they just wanted to get acquainted and consolidate with one another, you know. Get together, be together. But not nowadays.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, not nowadays, you know. They, well, I don't know. The world has changed, that's all I can say. And big change. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've heard it all before, haven't you?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well,

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I wish I'd get rid of that coffin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, growing up, more of the family would take care of it, right?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, that's right. Well, them days, you know, because they'd go down and call the doctor and he'd come out. Well, the neighbors found out just they could come over and see what they could do. Well, if they could do anything, they would done it. Sure. Any medicine they gave tonight, they'd, two would come tonight, two tomorrow night, and they'd all, that way they'd give you your medicine, you know.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: But now you gotta go to hospital.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No wonder they're living long, is it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: But they're charging you for it, too. That's for sure. That's for sure. We're paying for all of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, like I was thinking, too, like when you were working in the woods, then you and Herschel, you worked together a fair bit, didn't you?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, we worked, well, Herschel and I run together for, well, it was anywhere one way or the other than it was. Working, you know, working together. And then he went to the Navy and then I couldn't go home. I didn't pass. I had a harness. And that's about all the time we separated from the time we come here to the present. All the time. All his work together. I'm thinking, I'm thinking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, was that, was that common for, for the, for guys around here to work with a relative and, you know, stick with them?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, well, he wasn't my relatives then, you know, we just chums. Oh, it was before, before he married, uh, the Ebola. Yeah, that was before he married. I know that you and I can work together. And then, his brother, Shorty, he was a cook and I worked for him up there in the camp three years one time. I thought that then I done second work for him a while there.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So, we always got along pretty good. I wonder why you and Herschel hit it off so good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why, why you got good friends?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, we come here and we, I tell you, we come here and we bought the place where they lived. So we moved on that. And they went down just across the road and bought another place off the company. Well, I've bought a close neighbor's, see. We've been close neighbors ever since.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, that's the way we got to be close. Close.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of, of flunking in the, in the camp? Did you like that work?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I didn't like work inside. Wasn't too bad in the wintertime, but it lowered in the summertime. Hot, grease run out of you, hmm. So, it wasn't too good. But, I was working in there and when I went to take examination in World War I, I weighed 189 pounds. So, I gained a little weight in there.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That was something peculiar. I took a, what was it, 300 and something, we were at Moscow one day for examination. And at eleven I was didn't pass. So, they kept us in Moscow from Friday, we got out, Friday evening till Monday night. And they shipped us eleven of us to Lewiston. And they had eleven doctors down there. Monday night, he was going over. They all passed but me. I never passed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, I was the last one going through the chute and he said, what do you think about it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I said, well, you only got one to die. I guess you as well get killed in a war of any place. He just kind of laughed and said, you're taking a pretty good natured incident. I said, yep. Well, he said, I'll tell you, I ain't going to examine you very close. I know what's the matter. And he said, if you have to. He said, if they run too short a man, I'm going to put you in Special Limited Service number 1A. He said, now you can go in the Spruce Division. Over on the coast in airplanes. Well, I was working in the woods and so they took an ocean up there then in that old Camp 10. Boy, they had some beautiful white pine in there. So, we got out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was, what was it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Five, six cars. Airplane stock for the government. Well, I went and helped pick it out and we had to chop into every tree. And see if it was good straight grain, vertical grain before they dug it. Well, it was vertical grain why we marked it and we had to dig sawed things around and saw it. And they took another donkey, an old steam donkey and drug her in down the landing. You couldn't handle a set of tongs in it. You couldn't stick a PB in it. And to skid it, we had to put chains around it. We could daresn't use tongs. We could have scarred it up. That would have had a lot of time. We finally got it out and got it loaded. Of course, we made good money. Uncle Sam was paying us for that. So, I got a letter from him to be to Moscow. I believe it was Wednesday morning. He gets the train out of there for Seattle. If I want to go over to his Bruce division. Or if I didn't want to, they was going to get out some up there at Bovill and I was working right there and just stayed there and helped him. So, that's how I got the job there. So, I just stayed there. I was scaling walls at the time. And they put me in one of the cruisers. Picking it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, you never went over there for the Spruce division?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, I never went to the Spruce division. No. I kind of wish several times I had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why so?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, I learned there was logging in the woods over on the coast. We logged a little different here than they did. So, I learned something else there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did you feel, did you care at the time about whether you went in or not? Did you want to go in the army then?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, it didn't make no difference. I figured this way, you only got once a day and you're going to go. If you're born to be shot in the army, you're born to be shot in the army. And if you're not, well, you're out of it. That's the way I looked at it. So, I didn't go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did all them guys who were like them Austrians, did a lot of them go back to the old country then during the natural war?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: A lot of them went to the army.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They went to the army?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, you bet. For the United States. They didn't like Austria. They were a little too strict over there. They couldn't make the money they wanted to, you know. No way to make it. Of course, it was pretty thick to settle, I guess, anyhow, the way they talked. And what I gathered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were real poor over there, weren't they?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah. And boy, you know, come over here, a bunch of them, send over a bunch, you know. Maybe 35 or 40 of them, one herd. Boy, the way they would eat for a few days. Boy, you couldn't keep enough on the table.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Get them filled up, it's all right. Boy, I felt sorry for them.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Just the way they ate. You know, they had to be hungry. Or they wouldn't have ate that much. I think I put some in. You better look.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe it's burned out, has it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Pretty well. I've heard some things about those guys that aren't too kind, not too complimentary. Like, you know, like they were stupid. You know, and I don't know why they would be stupid of anybody else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time did you have your breakfast?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time did you have your breakfast?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Some guy told me that they were probably the worst bunch from that part of Europe. They were trying to get rid of them so they came over here. I guess they were pretty rough over there. I guess their government was pretty rough on them. The way I gathered it, I didn't come right out and ask any of them to find out. But I just gathered it the way they talked. The time that they had over there trying to live. The government they had there was pretty close on them. I think they were a little too close.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Did you ever hear, I don't know if you did or not, but there was one story about Big Red getting killed in Elk River. Why, it kept pretty quiet. Because the old jacks, they didn't want to get tangled up in it. So they never said much about it. If they didn't talk about it, they couldn't get mixed up in it. Go ahead and settle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, well should I go up there and tell them you're a soldier?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: They said no, I'm not going to shut up. Well, this country's all changed. A lot. I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about guys getting killed?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Old guys. They got killed in the woods. Right out here, well it is north of the hen house. It used to be when I come here the cabin is there yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what done it, were money or what?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: But anyhow, the fella that owned the cabin, he was shot through the window one night. Well, they knowed it was after night because the coal oil light had burnt dry. And he was killed, shot through the window.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, I asked all the crew and couldn't find out nothing at all. Here after I built the house. Well, ground out there, there still was a dugout for the log house. It's still there, the whole.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: One Sunday there was a couple married couple young folks. I had a little kid with them when I was four. She was five or six years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she said, where did that log cabin used to stand?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I said right there. It was only about twenty feet from one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said, where was that leaning pine tree?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I said, the stump level is right there. She said, yeah. Looks alright. And she was from Canada. And she said that used to was her uncle's homestead cabin, nothing there. So it must have been her uncle got killed. Now she didn't say in there, I didn't ask her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, was you here when the man got killed?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No. It was before. It was before my time quite a bit. All I got to get, you know, hearsay and talk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there hearsay about who might have done it at the time?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Never heard.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: The Warren Staff place up here, I don't know how many times it's been so, Herschel, or Herschel, or not Herschel, but, my son -in -law has got it now, the Warren Staff place.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: this Warren Staff, he sold some timber or stumpage or something. And he had about, oh, twenty -eight or nine hundred dollars or something like that on them. They thought, they never found the money, but they found him, down here on the main road, oh about, probably two and a half, his horse come home, but he wasn't on it. They went right down the road and were looking for him and they found him. They found him laying there where he had been shot. But there's no money on him. So, some fella cleaned him, you know. I'm sure it's not going to end fast. You just didn't get a crime. How much it had just come in now. This is like, the story that I heard about Bovill burning down, and the Fourth of July, I think, 1914 it was, the story about that was, a man had gotten, quite a bit of money out of the bank, and a fella, killed him, stole his money, set the fire in the hotel, to cover it up, burned the whole street, main street. Yeah, burned the whole thing up. Yeah. Well, he started quite a fire here. I was here that year.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, he started quite a fire when he burnt that.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yes, for that. You know. And I haven't had to. Well,

speaker: Dick Benge
words: one thing now, they don't have the fire they used to have, but, they used to have some good ones. Fires. I don't know, they were in the 14th. We, OTP Jones, he's superintendent, and he loaded, I think, a fat carload of us, and went up, then walked the track north of Bovill. So he ordered us all off. Well, someone got off, some of us didn't. We could see the fire coming over the hill, and boys, he was just a -boiling. The first thing we know, we looked across the track on the other hill, and the fire was burning over there. So, we all ended off the train. God, OTP got mad. Oh, boy, he got mad.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Old Bill Helmer come along, and he said, he said, I ordered them off, and they won't get off. He said, I ordered them to stay on.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: By God, he said, I'm running this. He said, maybe you're running part of it, but he said, by God, you ain't running at all. He said, I ain't gonna turn the crew loose here and get them worked up. Hell, he said, in a half an hour, that fire will be clear across the railroad tracks there. Right with us. Oh, no. He said, down there in front of her, but I'm moving this damn train out of here. Man and all. Old TV, he chewed his tongue, but he had to get on the train there, too.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, that burnt the old fella up, because he was wood -super -dramatic, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, he was better higher up than old Helmer was, then?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, he thought he was.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Old Helmer showed him by God, he wasn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the same fire that almost burned Bovill up?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. The same one, well, we went out north of Bovill up, afternoon.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: We could see the fire coming across over the hill, and she was a -comin'.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So, that old Bill Helmer, you know, he was the head of ODP. When it come to knowing things in the woods. And he's level -headed. He had a reason to him. Jones had no bit more reason to him than that dog. Not a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would Jones get, you know, to be the head of the woods when he didn't know about it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He had a way to get old Jones, he'd take them god damn crazy spells. He went down to that old camp level one time, and old, he was gonna show him how to log. He logged there one day, and went back down next morning. Old Tom Malady, he was running camp. The boys was standing around, the horses standing around, their heads down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: OTP said, Tom, he said, what's the matter?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: They ain't doing much today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The damn fool come out from town yesterday and tried to show him how to log, and said, ain't they logged today?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, he'd get old every now and then. But he just snapped it that way then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He, they wasn't logging because they was logging his way? Was that it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, he didn't know any crazy spells.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Just messin' everything on him went and go his way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear the story about him helpin' burn down the burden, Gus Burden's place in Bovill?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, I read it. That's why, yeah, I've heard it. I wonder about it. I think he did. But he didn't like that place.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He figured that they worked against him the whole time. Well, I guess they did on the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because what made him that over them, you know?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Why, when Wobbly struck, I -W -W, why, old Gus, he protected him. He said, made old T .P. mad. Of course, Gus, well, he had that business there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Reckon he had a right to run it through himself, didn't he?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Even wasn't Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think it was he done?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He gave him a place to meet, a place to be. Oh, they all got in there, Gus, and they'd play pool, and white pop and one thing or another, you know, and he... Yeah,

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I think so. I think that's a whole trouble. But it's in the book. I got that, where the trees grew tall. Yeah, that's where, yeah, and the fella in Bovill told me the same story. The guy that told it, you know, to Miller. You knew at the time that he didn't like virgins at all. He didn't like, you knew that. Yeah, I know he didn't.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, you know, we used to go to town and we'd get pretty full, some of us. And old Gus, he'd take us in and put us to bed. Boy, that made old T .P. mad. He didn't like that. He thought we'd oughta get out on the job. Well, maybe we should have. Down before Shaped to Work the way we was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was, where, where did you guys do most of the drinking then, when it was moonshine?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You know, you couldn't drink right in the saloon in town because they didn't have the saloons. You know. Oh, they'd get behind the building someplace. And old Pat Malone up there, you know, he didn't have it in there.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Because he didn't cure them. No, one time, old Pat and I, we used pretty good chums. And, uh, so, uh, I was pretty drunk there one time and we was going up the street and there was one on each side of me. Kinda thudded me. Old Pat said, tell you my friend's dead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One on each side?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. No, it was just lumberjacks helping me along, you know. So, that was alright, so I got by that time alright. Downtown, oh, about a week after that. Old Pat, you know, he was a bachelor and he stayed there at the hotel all the time. So, that was across the street. Old Pat said, hey Dick, come over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just bought some new furniture, why don't you go up and look at it?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah. Went up, the old fella had a case, a Canadian club, never been opened.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So, we pretty well went down on one bottle. I told him, by God Pat, I gotta get out of here, it's too hot in here now. Yeah, he said, I think we both better go. So, you take a cop like that, well, he's alright with the yak. Yeah, he didn't want a piece of no lumberjack. Not any old, but I can tell you one thing. If the old fella told you to do something, you better start doing it. If you didn't, by God, he'd see that you did.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I was a good old cop. Well, after he come down here to Princeton one night. I come down, no, I was down there. I laid off two weeks. Had a big dance while I was here. Geez, here come, I think there's poor cops and him from Moscow. Deputy Sheriff, Sheriff, all of them. So Pat, he kept sidling around doing, sidling around doing. I figured nobody wanted him. I was the only guy that got up close to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I got in a cold tonight, and I said, Crap, man, I'm freezing to death and not having real nice either, will you Pat?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I said, don't worry, man, get a drink. Well, I said, no, he can get a drink. So we'd go and go out. One of my chums there, he, I said, come on, East, let's go get a drink. We got about halfway down the steps, and he noticed me and pulled back on my arm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, you know that fella wearing a star?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I said, give a damn if you got on two of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, do you know him?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, hell yeah, I do. I said, Pat, I don't know him. I'm better going to him than I am you. So he said, Jesus Christ, I don't know him. Well, I said, let's go take a chance. So we went down. So we gave the fella a drink, and I gave East a drink, and took one myself.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: So I had Pat to take another one before he went back. But Jesus talked stuff, I don't know whether he should have run through the still three or four more times. He just made it to sell. Boy, that was the powerful stuff that ever I drunk. But we got by with it. Old Pat, he drank it and smacked his lips and never frowned on a bit. Boy.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, I run into a fella, worked in the woods with him there from Wisconsin, Minnesota. He knowed old Pat back there, and he said, I knowed old Pat, he said, before he ever got to be sheriff. And he told me then, he said, I can tell you a whole lot about old Pat.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: He said, the niggers got into it there one time, and they killed a couple of them, the niggers themselves. And they sent the star on old Pat and told him to go up and get him. My God, he said he went up and got him. He said he's wore a star ever since I've knowed him ever since. But if he told you to do anything, you better do it. That's just what he meant.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Crutch -old boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Speaking of Negroes, did you ever know the Wales family? The niggers?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Hell yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Chuck and what were the other boys? Roy?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Roy and Chuck. Joe was the old man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they like?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Very nice people. Nice people you ever see.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: I've eaten their house. And she was as good a cook as I ever had after. Joe, he used to log all the time. While I was scaling there at the time of sex, and Joe, he was logging between Derry and the time of sex. So I wandered off my job one day and went up where Joe was logging. Just looking around. Old Joe said, Jesus Christ, Dick, he said, time to eat. Let's go eat. We wasn't too far from the house. I went over and had lunch with him.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: And, uh,

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Cougar Jack used to be there in Derry and I was just trying to think of his name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get a hold of his name?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, I've heard different things about him. Some say he was a real strong man. Oh my God, he was. You betcha he was strong. Cougar Jack and Chuck got drunk in Derry one day. Well, they were going down the street. Joe, he had one arm around Old Jack and Jack had his and one arm around Old Chuck. And they were trying to hold one another up, Old Nigger Joe. And get by. So, get down there in the street and Old Joe, he stopped and looked up and down the street. By God, he sat there in with two white men in Derry.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: That was him and Old Cougar Jack.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Chuck and Roy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to know Chuck and Roy, them brothers at all, working in the woods?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No, I never worked with them, but they worked in a store there at Helmer. Well, I think they owned one. That's where I got acquainted with them. I don't know, I guess they don't run the store there anymore. Oh, I don't know if it's open at all. I mean, I guess too, I thought they sold gas there a couple of years ago. Maybe it's shut down since then. They're not in it anymore. They're all gone.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yep. So, Old Jones was really a bag of wind. Oh, yee -jib. You know, I've heard he had a lot of ideas about how everybody should do things immoral and all that. I mean, he had a funny idea about being strict with men and that kind of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know Laird? Who? Laird?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Oh, yeah. He was here at Wild House.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of him?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: There was a reasonable man. Laird. He reasoned. Re reasoned anything right out with you. If he was the fault, he thought. If you was the fault, he'd get the two ideas together and he'd talk it over with you. And that's what I call a man. I don't know, first experience we ever had. With him we had a two -year -old colt and he turned it out on the range and then the runner on the trussle up that platter killed it.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, a session foreman came up that night and told us what had happened. He'd been up and buried it that day and he told us ours and had our brand on it. Well, Dad went down and seen Laird and seen him about it. Well, I said, I don't know. Mr. Ben, he said, you was a pastor in our land once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, oh, you bought some of us, why don't you fence it and pastor that?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Well, Dad said, you've rung up a good question.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, why don't you fence your damn railroad track?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yes, he said, you've got a point there. And laughed, you know, took it all good -nightly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, he said, well, how would $35 be for the horse?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Of course, horses in that time weren't worth much. Dad said, you take $35 and call us square and have everything settled up. So, he wrote him out a check for $35 for the colt.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: No arguing, no nothing. Just going to terms. But you know, a man like that can run something and get by with it. Well, old Jones, oh, he'd have blowed up and ugh. Yeah, I know him. Well, I've heard some people say that Laird was kind of an arrogant man. I mean, that he was, he considered himself way above most of the people. Well, I never figured him that way and I've talked to him lots. He'd come out and talk to you right on the job.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: And I never figured him that way. Of course, he'd come out and ask you and ask you how your job was and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What could you do to help us?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: You know, we can get everybody's ideas. He didn't just take off. Well, I know him. He'd go ahead and get all the ideas.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: They had one down there, Jim O 'Connell's boy. He run things and he'd worry that he'd run right or not, he'd run them. So, he made some of the damnedest mistakes.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Trying to run it.

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Yeah, he took me up the river one time and he wanted to put in a catwalk and run the logs down into the hot pond. I told him that. Well, he went up the river. I took the pile driver up the day before. He told me where to leave it and I left it to her. So, we went on about our business and he came out. And on that day I was sleuthing logs up there. There for fun. And he came up and he'd run a new man with him and he said, I'll put these two men here in a rota -filing for catwalks to run the logs in the hot pond. Well, we went up and looked it over and I told him about where he'd have to put it. If he wanted to run the logs in the hot pond. Well, he wouldn't put it there. You know, he put it down the river about a hundred feet. Well, that he got us. I put it down and all the logs were going the wrong way and I hit the sluicing gap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hit the what?

speaker: Dick Benge
words: Sluicing gap. Hit the sluicing gap crossways. Well, you couldn't get a log through its crossways. So, he brought me two men up and he said, I wish you could go down and see how many logs you can get through that. I went down and I worked not too hard, but I worked. I worked about twenty minutes I guess and I guess I got four logs through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, you got a PV?

speaker: Dick Benge
